perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be construed, interpreted and governed by and in accordance with the laws of Hong Kong. In case the arbitration proceedings fail, an unresolved dispute between Developer and VAL is subject to the binding laws of Hong Kong as a first attempt at formal resolution. Should arbitration fail to reach a resolution and either party wish to pursue the dispute further, this shall be conducted within the binding laws of Hong Kong.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be construed, interpreted and governed at the sole discretion of VGrab Asia Ltd. In case the arbitration proceedings fail, VGrab Asia Ltd. will determine the course of action.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the explicit application of Hong Kong law, giving VGrab Asia Ltd. complete control over dispute resolution, contradicting Hong Kong's arbitration ordinance which protects the rights of both parties in a commercial agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text eliminates the application of Hong Kong law and provides VGrab with full dispute resolution control. This contradicts the Arbitration Ordinance (Cap. 609) which requires a fair and impartial process for resolving disputes. Section 3 states that the purpose of the Ordinance is to facilitate fair resolution of disputes by arbitration without unnecessary delay or expense.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Hong Kong e-Legislation Loading required resource. Please wait
perturbation.justification: The changed text eliminates the application of Hong Kong law, granting VGrab Asia Ltd. complete control over dispute resolution. This directly contradicts the Arbitration Ordinance as explained by the LLM, which requires a fair and impartial process. The removal of the jurisdiction and applicable laws in the changed text, replacing it with sole discretion, constitutes a clear omission of legally mandated protections, aligning with the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states 'Hong Kong e-Legislation Loading required resource. Please wait', which, while incomplete, indicates the existence of Hong Kong legislation that would typically govern such agreements.

perturbation.original_text: All Intellectual Property during the project is owned by VAL, and will be turned over to VAL at the conclusion of the project by Developer and after the fulfillment of all commercial obligations by the VAL. All rights and title to Duesey Coffee Intellectual Property created pursuant to the Project shall belong to VAL and shall be subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: All Intellectual Property created during the project is owned by VAL. All rights and title to Duesey Coffee Intellectual Property created pursuant to the Project shall belong to VAL.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text removes the clause requiring the developer to turn over the Intellectual Property after the fulfillment of all commercial obligations by VAL. This creates a possible scenario where the developer hands over the IP even if VAL hasn't paid them, potentially violating the implied right to payment for work done as protected in Hong Kong's contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Hong Kong Contract Law - implied terms
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text specifies that the IP transfer occurs after VAL meets its commercial obligations. Omitting this could imply the IP must be transferred regardless of payment, contradicting the implied terms related to payment for services under Hong Kong's contract law. While not explicitly codified, this principle is upheld in case law and interpretations of contract law. The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Ordinance (Cap. 623) does not directly deal with implied terms but is relevant to contract law in HK.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Hong Kong e-Legislation Loading required resource. Please wait
perturbation.justification: The original contract text ensures the developer transfers Intellectual Property (IP) to VAL only after VAL fulfills its commercial obligations. The changed text omits this condition, potentially forcing the developer to transfer IP regardless of whether VAL has met its payment obligations. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this omission could violate the implied right to payment for work done under Hong Kong's contract law. The Law Explanation supports this by noting that the original text's condition protects against IP transfer before payment, and removing it could contradict implied payment terms. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is empty and Snippet 1 is a placeholder, the explanations strongly argue for a contradiction based on contract law principles in Hong Kong, specifically regarding payment for services rendered. This aligns with the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction', where omitting essential information (the condition for IP transfer) creates a legal loophole that potentially harms the developer's right to payment.

perturbation.original_text: In case Developer terminates the agreement, it shall handover the entire project related IPR, work done till date to VAL.
perturbation.changed_text: In case Developer terminates the agreement, VAL maintains full rights over project related IPR, irrespective of work completion status.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text makes it absolute that VAL maintains the rights over IPR if the developer terminates the agreement, irrespective of the work done or payments made. This contradicts the Hong Kong's contract law, that requires fair compensations in case of termination for the work completed and rights of the developer.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Hong Kong Contract Law - Unjust Enrichment
perturbation.law_citation: Restatement (Third) of Restitution and Unjust Enrichment § 1
perturbation.law_explanation: This change eliminates any obligation for VAL to compensate the developer for the work already done if the developer terminates. Hong Kong contract law, based on common law principles, generally prevents unjust enrichment. While there isn't a specific statute, the principle is well-established in case law. The Restatement (Third) of Restitution and Unjust Enrichment § 1 from US law provides a reference to unjust enrichment as the VAL could profit unfairly from the developer's work without compensation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: unjust enrichment | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you unjust enrichment Unjust enrichment occurs when Party A confers a benefit upon Party B without Party A receiving the proper restitution required by law. This typically occurs in a contractual agreement when Party A fulfills their part of the agreement and Party B does not fulfill their part of the agreement. Unjust enrichment is distinguished from a gift , as a gift is given without the reasonable expectation of receiving something in return. As such, when Party A gives Party B a gift, Party A has no legal recourse to receive something in return. Recovery Under a Claim of Unjust Enrichment To recover on a claim of unjust enrichment, the plaintiff must show that the defendant was unjustly enriched at the plaintiff's expense. Therefore, according to Bloomgarden v. Coyer , the plaintiff has the burden of proof. Recovery on a theory of unjust enrichment typically occurs where there was no contract between the parties, or a contract turns out to be invalid. See: quasi-contract . Guiding Principles There are two principles which help to refine the circumstances under which a plaintiff cannot bring an unjust enrichment claim: Gift principle The plaintiff cannot give the defendant a gift, and then sue the defendant, under unjust enrichment for not giving anything in return. Choice principle The plaintiff cannot confer a benefit upon the defendant without giving the defendant the choice to reject the benefit, and then expect something in return from the defendant. Example: The plaintiff cannot paint the defendant's house in the middle of the night when the defendant is sleeping, and then expect the defendant to pay the plaintiff for the plaintiff's efforts (assuming that the two parties had not contracted for this service to be performed at this time). [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords contract law Wex COMMERCE commercial activities wex articles business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts legal principles of unjust enrichment. The original text ensures handover of IPR and work done to VAL if the Developer terminates the agreement. The changed text, however, allows VAL to maintain full rights over project-related IPR, irrespective of work completion status, potentially without compensating the developer for work already performed. This contradiction aligns with the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction,' as it removes the developer's right to fair compensation, a legally mandated consideration. The Law Explanation notes that Hong Kong contract law, based on common law principles, generally prevents unjust enrichment. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines unjust enrichment as occurring 'when Party A confers a benefit upon Party B without Party A receiving the proper restitution required by law.' By allowing VAL to retain IPR without compensating the developer, the modified contract creates a situation where VAL could be unjustly enriched at the developer's expense, violating the legal principle of fair compensation for work completed.
